data.title: Be Sure To Check Out Token Corporation (TSE:1766) Before It Goes Ex-Dividend
data.description:
It looks like Token Corporation (TSE:1766) is about to go ex-dividend in the next four days. The ex-dividend date is usually set to be two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date on which you must be present on the company's books as a shareholder in order to receive the dividend. The ex-dividend date is important as the process of settlement involves at least two full business days. So if you miss that date, you would not show up on the company's books on the record date. This means that investors who purchase Token's shares on or after the 28th of April will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 30th of July.

The company's next dividend payment will be JP¥360.00 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed JP¥360 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Token has a trailing yield of 2.7% on the current share price of JP¥13440.00. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

If a company pays out more in dividends than it earned, then the dividend might become unsustainable - hardly an ideal situation. That's why it's good to see Token paying out a modest 27% of its earnings. Yet cash flows are even more important than profits for assessing a dividend, so we need to see if the company generated enough cash to pay its distribution. What's good is that dividends were well covered by free cash flow, with the company paying out 22% of its cash flow last year.

It's positive to see that Token's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

View our latest analysis for Token

Click here to see how much of its profit Token paid out over the last 12 months.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. For this reason, we're glad to see Token's earnings per share have risen 14% per annum over the last five years. Earnings per share are growing rapidly and the company is keeping more than half of its earnings within the business; an attractive combination which could suggest the company is focused on reinvesting to grow earnings further. Fast-growing businesses that are reinvesting heavily are enticing from a dividend perspective, especially since they can often increase the payout ratio later.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. Token has delivered 14% dividend growth per year on average over the past 10 years. It's great to see earnings per share growing rapidly over several years, and dividends per share growing right along with it.

The Bottom Line

Is Token an attractive dividend stock, or better left on the shelf? We love that Token is growing earnings per share while simultaneously paying out a low percentage of both its earnings and cash flow. These characteristics suggest the company is reinvesting in growing its business, while the conservative payout ratio also implies a reduced risk of the dividend being cut in the future. Token looks solid on this analysis overall, and we'd definitely consider investigating it more closely.

data.title: Analysts Are Updating Their OBIC Co.,Ltd. (TSE:4684) Estimates After Its Full-Year Results
data.description:
Investors in OBIC Co.,Ltd. (TSE:4684) had a good week, as its shares rose 3.3% to close at JP¥4,264 following the release of its annual results. OBICLtd reported JP¥135b in revenue, roughly in line with analyst forecasts, although statutory earnings per share (EPS) of JP¥172 beat expectations, being 2.9% higher than what the analysts expected. Following the result, the analysts have updated their earnings model, and it would be good to know whether they think there's been a strong change in the company's prospects, or if it's business as usual. We thought readers would find it interesting to see the analysts latest (statutory) post-earnings forecasts for next year.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Taking into account the latest results, the consensus forecast from OBICLtd's twelve analysts is for revenues of JP¥148.0b in 2027. This reflects a solid 9.4% improvement in revenue compared to the last 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are predicted to rise 5.8% to JP¥184. In the lead-up to this report, the analysts had been modelling revenues of JP¥147.6b and earnings per share (EPS) of JP¥182 in 2027. So it's pretty clear that, although the analysts have updated their estimates, there's been no major change in expectations for the business following the latest results.

Check out our latest analysis for OBICLtd

It will come as no surprise then, to learn that the consensus price target is largely unchanged at JP¥4,904. That's not the only conclusion we can draw from this data however, as some investors also like to consider the spread in estimates when evaluating analyst price targets. There are some variant perceptions on OBICLtd, with the most bullish analyst valuing it at JP¥5,800 and the most bearish at JP¥4,100 per share. There are definitely some different views on the stock, but the range of estimates is not wide enough as to imply that the situation is unforecastable, in our view.

These estimates are interesting, but it can be useful to paint some more broad strokes when seeing how forecasts compare, both to the OBICLtd's past performance and to peers in the same industry. We can infer from the latest estimates that forecasts expect a continuation of OBICLtd'shistorical trends, as the 9.4% annualised revenue growth to the end of 2027 is roughly in line with the 9.8% annual growth over the past five years. Compare this with the broader industry, which analyst estimates (in aggregate) suggest will see revenues grow 5.0% annually. So it's pretty clear that OBICLtd is forecast to grow substantially faster than its industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The most important thing to take away is that there's been no major change in sentiment, with the analysts reconfirming that the business is performing in line with their previous earnings per share estimates. Fortunately, they also reconfirmed their revenue numbers, suggesting that it's tracking in line with expectations. Additionally, our data suggests that revenue is expected to grow faster than the wider industry. The consensus price target held steady at JP¥4,904, with the latest estimates not enough to have an impact on their price targets.

With that in mind, we wouldn't be too quick to come to a conclusion on OBICLtd. Long-term earnings power is much more important than next year's profits. We have estimates - from multiple OBICLtd analysts - going out to 2029, and you can see them free on our platform here.

data.title: Federal Scrutiny Of Duke Energy Dams Reframes Long Term Risk Story
data.description:
• Federal regulators are increasing safety oversight at key Duke Energy (NYSE:DUK) hydro facilities.
  • Seismic remediation work at the Nantahala Dam and updated risk assessments at the Jocassee project are central to the new requirements.
  • The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission is pressing for specific compliance and engineering actions tied to dam safety.

Duke Energy, trading around $125.25, operates in a sector where reliable infrastructure and regulatory relationships are critical to long term operations. The stock’s return of 54.3% over the past 5 years and 43.2% over 3 years indicates that many investors have treated NYSE:DUK as a core utility holding. These new dam safety actions add an extra layer for you to consider in addition to the usual income and stability narrative around the company.

For investors watching NYSE:DUK, the key questions will likely center on how seismic remediation and updated risk assessments affect project timelines, required capital and operating flexibility. The increased FERC involvement suggests that operational discipline and transparent safety reporting could be just as important as any long term grid or community plans when you assess Duke’s risk profile.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Duke Energy by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Duke Energy.

Is Duke Energy's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

The new Federal Energy Regulatory Commission requirements bring Duke Energy’s hydro assets into sharper focus for anyone tracking operational risk. At Nantahala, regulators are asking for more rigorous engineering work, including potential non linear modeling, full Finite Element Analysis inputs and outputs, and detailed construction logistics such as reservoir drawdown timing and gate availability during floods. That adds complexity to project planning and could affect capital allocation or schedules if designs need to be revised. At Jocassee, FERC has granted more time for updated risk analysis, which removes immediate safety pressure but keeps regulatory review active. For you, the takeaway is that FERC is not questioning the dams’ current safety but is insisting on more detailed proof and controls before construction advances. This kind of scrutiny can limit near term flexibility yet may reduce the risk of unexpected remediation demands later if Duke satisfies the conditions fully.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Duke Energy Narrative

  • The detailed seismic remediation work and extra documentation requirements line up with the narrative theme that Duke relies on long term, capital intensive infrastructure where strong regulatory relationships and safety performance support continued approvals.
  • The tighter engineering expectations and extra design work could challenge the narrative’s focus on execution by adding timing and cost uncertainty around hydro projects, especially alongside large grid and generation plans for peers such as NextEra Energy and Southern Company.
  • The specific need to justify modeling assumptions, provide comprehensive FEA data and submit construction emergency plans is not fully reflected in the current narrative, which concentrates more on demand growth, legislation and large scale investment than on project level safety compliance steps.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Duke Energy to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ FERC’s requests for additional analysis, design details and construction planning point to potential project delays or cost adjustments if Duke needs to revise models or methods to satisfy regulators.
  • ⚠️ The need for a Temporary Construction Emergency Action Plan, quality control program and extra approvals for structures like cofferdams adds layers of compliance that sit on top of existing balance sheet and interest coverage concerns analysts have already flagged.
  • 🎁 FERC’s statement that the Jocassee extension will not negatively affect current project safety suggests there is time to address comments without immediate operational disruption, which may help Duke phase work alongside other investments.
  • 🎁 Clear regulatory expectations around design packages and safety documentation can reduce the risk of future surprises if Duke completes the required work, giving investors more visibility on how these hydro assets fit into the broader utility portfolio.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how quickly Duke responds to FERC’s comment resolution timeline and whether future filings show any change in construction schedules, project budgets or dam operating parameters. Pay attention to updates on Nantahala’s final design package, particularly around seismic modeling choices, and to whether the revised Jocassee risk analysis leads to further conditions or remains routine. Any sign that these hydro safety reviews spill over into broader capital plans, rate requests or financing needs will be especially important for anyone treating Duke Energy as a core, lower volatility holding.
